main_summary.summary: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland, interviewed by John Dittmer in Arlington, Virginia on March 17, 2013, recounts her path from a segregated Arlington childhood and Georgia summers at her grandmother's house in Ocoeenee through six decades of civil rights work. She traces her religious and constitutional formation in Presbyterian youth fellowship, secret integrated meetings held while the American Nazi Party operated nearby, and the influence of seeing the segregated black school near her grandmother's Wayne County, Georgia home. As a Duke freshman she joined the Durham sit-ins with North Carolina College students, was twice jailed, and was nearly expelled. Moving to Washington in 1960, she joined the Howard-based Nonviolent Action Group (NAG), participating in sit-ins in Arlington, the summer-long Glen Echo Amusement Park picket with Bannockburn community support, and a Rock Hill, South Carolina support action. In May 1961 she rode the Freedom Rides to Jackson, Mississippi, was arrested by Captain J.L. Ray, held in Hinds County Jail and Parchman Penitentiary's death row for two months, and then enrolled at Tougaloo College as one of its first full-time white students. She participated in the May 28, 1963 Jackson Woolworth's sit-in with Anne Moody, Memphis Norman, Pearlena Lewis, John R. Salter Jr., and Lois Chaffee, worked in the March on Washington press office, and survived a near-fatal Klan attack near Canton in June 1964 with Edwin King and Hamid Kizilbash.
key_themes: White Southern entry into the civil rights movement
Nonviolent Action Group (NAG) and Howard student organizing
Freedom Rides and Parchman Penitentiary incarceration
Tougaloo College integration and Jackson Movement
Klan and American Nazi Party violence and intimidation
main_summary.historical_significance:
Mulholland's testimony provides first-person documentation of multiple movement nodes rarely covered together in a single oral history: the Durham sit-ins, the Washington/Howard NAG cohort, the Glen Echo Amusement Park desegregation campaign, the Freedom Rides arrival in Jackson, Parchman's death-row women's wing, the iconic Jackson Woolworth's sit-in photographed by Fred Blackwell, and the 1964 Canton ambush that Edwin King has identified as a plot to stop Freedom Summer. She names participants others have not (Reverend C.A. Ivory, Frank Holloway, Hamid Kizilbash, Pearlena Lewis, Memphis Norman, Rabbi Perry Nussbaum, Clarie Collins Harvey, Jack H. Young Sr., Carsie Hall) and gives detail on Women Power Unlimited's logistical support that does not appear elsewhere in the LoC CRHP corpus.

Her position as a Southern white woman moving between communities yields observations specific to her vantage point: the calibration of Parchman matrons toward white prisoners, the cultural distance she felt from Northern white Freedom Riders, the kneeling Stokely Carmichael who met her young twins at a Smithsonian event, and the Smithsonian morgue-worker testimony she heard about James Chaney's body. The interview is essential for researchers of NAG, the Tougaloo cohort, and white Southern movement participation.

chapters.title: Arlington Childhood and Georgia Roots
chapters.summary: Mulholland describes being born in Washington, D.C. and raised in Arlington's Buckingham apartments among New York Jewish neighbors during the early 1940s. She introduces her father from southwest Iowa working at the Department of Labor and her Georgia-born mother, frames Arlington as segregated by law and custom, and describes annual summer visits to her grandmother in Ocoeenee, Georgia, a logging-camp company town in Wayne County.

chapters.title: Presbyterian Conscience and Secret Meetings
chapters.summary: Mulholland recounts memorizing Bible verses and the Declaration of Independence, attending a segregated Presbyterian youth fellowship in Arlington that hosted secret integrated meetings with black YWCA students despite the American Nazi Party headquartered around the corner. She describes seeing the Niggertown school disparity in Georgia and a Spanish classmate displaced by Virginia's massive resistance school closures as formative motivations.

chapters.title: Duke and the Durham Sit-Ins
chapters.summary: At Duke University as a 1959 freshman, Mulholland and roommate Lucia rejected sorority rush and joined the Durham sit-ins after North Carolina College students visited the Presbyterian chaplain's secret meeting. She was arrested twice, jailed under a plan exploiting North Carolina's 'others' racial category that attorney Floyd McKissick judged frivolous, and faced the Dean's locked-office demand that she telephone her segregationist mother.

chapters.title: NAG, Arlington Sit-Ins, and Glen Echo
chapters.summary: Mulholland dropped out of Duke, worked Capitol Hill jobs in Washington, and joined the Nonviolent Action Group at Howard, identified by NCC students as the lapsed founding-meeting affiliate. She describes integrated sit-ins at Drug Fair and Peoples in Cherrydale Arlington with American Nazi Party counter-pickets and the summer-long Glen Echo Amusement Park picket campaign supported by the Bannockburn, MD Jewish cooperative community.

chapters.title: Rock Hill Jail-In and Freedom Rides Origin
chapters.summary: Mulholland traveled with NAG to Rock Hill, South Carolina in support of the jail-in led by Reverend C.A. Ivory, where Frank Holloway was incarcerated and reporting for the Afro-American. She traces the Freedom Ride concept to a Greyhound conversation between Tom Gaither and Diane Nash after Rock Hill, the April SNCC Charlotte meeting calling for testing the December 1960 Supreme Court ruling, and the May 1961 Aniston bus burning that triggered NAG and Nashville deployment.

chapters.title: Freedom Ride to Jackson and Hinds County Jail
chapters.summary: Mulholland flew to New Orleans with a NAG Freedom Rider cohort, took the train to Jackson, and was arrested by Jackson Police Captain J.L. Ray on breach-of-peace charges under the Kennedy-Mississippi bargain to avoid Alabama-style violence. She describes the scripted trial, the overcrowded Hinds County women's cell where she had more in common with Southern black inmates than Northern white riders, and the Clarie Collins Harvey-founded Women Power Unlimited's church-cell supply network.

chapters.title: Parchman Penitentiary
chapters.summary: Transferred to Parchman's death row, Mulholland describes the intake vaginal exam by a matron and a friend's 45-year scent-triggered memory of it, the segregated cell-by-cell layout that prevented seeing other riders, Rabbi Perry Nussbaum's weekly Hebrew-prayer news smuggling that she preferred over the visiting Catholic seminarians, attorneys Jack H. Young Sr. and Carsie Hall barred from bringing William Kunstler, and her decision to serve out the two-month sentence rather than bail to fill the jails.

chapters.title: Tougaloo College Integration
chapters.summary: Mulholland enrolled at Tougaloo College in fall 1961, applying in reaction to Charlayne Hunter-Gault and Hamilton E. Holmes facing riots at Georgia. She describes Tougaloo's pre-segregation charter, Charlotte Phillips as the first white student preceding her, the dormitory girl who mistook her for a ghost, the AKA member's eventual acceptance, and her co-chairing of the campus NAACP nonviolent agitation group with Joyce Ladner.

chapters.title: Jackson Woolworth's Sit-In
chapters.summary: On May 28, 1963 Mulholland and dorm mother Lois Chaffee began as picket-line spotters but joined Pearlena Lewis, Anne Moody, and Memphis Norman after the picket arrest. She describes Norman pulled off the stool and stomped, John R. Salter Jr. attacked with brass knuckles and mustard, three hours of mob violence with police laughing outside, Fred Blackwell's iconic photograph, Tougaloo president Adam Daniel Beittel sitting at the counter to negotiate safe passage, and the Supreme Court precedent (citing her Durham case) that prevented police from entering the store.

chapters.title: March on Washington and Birmingham Bombing
chapters.summary: Mulholland worked in the March on Washington press office that summer, expecting federal intervention to prevent the buses arriving and merchants boarding downtown windows against the American Nazi Party across the river. She camped on the return trip with Anne Moody and Edwin King along the Blue Ridge, attended the 16th Street Baptist Church funerals in Birmingham where she picked up glass shards and shotgun shells, and identifies that day as the worst in the movement.

chapters.title: Galloway Church Visits and Canton Attack
chapters.summary: Mulholland describes the integrated church-visit campaign at Galloway Memorial Methodist Church, the resignation of Reverend W.B. Selah, and the post-graduation June 1964 attack near Canton on a carload of Edwin King, Hamid Kizilbash, and herself returning from a mass meeting. She identifies the attack as a planned Klan murder to stop Freedom Summer, deflected when Kizilbash was identified as Pakistani and the lead attacker pulled the others off, sending the violence instead toward Goodman, Schwerner, and Chaney days later.

chapters.title: Meredith March, Stokely, and Five Decades After
chapters.summary: Mulholland returned for the 1966 Meredith March, picking it up at Tougaloo and slipping the first printed 'Black Power' flyers into her New Testament. She describes Dean Branch's Tougaloo house hosting the Marlon Brando breakfast and Stokely Carmichael kneeling at a 1970s Smithsonian event to greet her young twin sons. She closes summarizing her menial post-graduation jobs, raising five sons, 30 years as a classroom teaching assistant, and her son Loki Mulholland's documentary 'An Ordinary Hero.'

loc_video.caption: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland oral history interview conducted by John Dittmer in Arlington, Virginia, 2013 March 17
